summary: Paul Graham argues that writing with ordinary words and simple sentences makes ideas easier to absorb, keeps readers engaged longer, and is more considerate to a global audience. Simple writing also exposes weak thinking, lasts better over time, and ultimately reflects good craftsmanship. He writes simply because complexity feels clumsy to him, and he achieves it through fast drafting and extensive cutting during revision.
key_points: Lower reading friction lets readers invest more energy in the ideas and finish the piece.
Simple writing is more respectful to readers, including non-native English speakers.
Fancy prose can hide a lack of ideas, while simple prose keeps the writer honest.
Plain style ages better as language and culture change.
He writes simply through rapid drafting followed by days of editing and cutting.
money_quotes: I try to write using ordinary words and simple sentences.
The less energy they expend on your prose, the more they'll have left for your ideas.
When you write in a fancy way to impress people, you're making them do extra work just so you can seem cool.
Whereas writing simply keeps you honest.
Indeed, lasting is not merely an accidental quality of chairs, or writing. It's a sign you did a good job.
key_insight: Clear, simple prose lowers friction, respects readers, and reveals the true strength of your ideas.
